title: Backup Window Guide
summary: Understand dataset size, change rate, backup type, effective throughput, compression/deduplication, overhead, and whether protection timing fits the available operating window.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool estimates whether backup work can complete in a practical time window after data change, throughput, savings, and overhead are considered.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid.
Use it to document assumptions before implementation.
Use it to catch design pressure before it becomes field trouble.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it when planning backup jobs, archive copies, offsite replication, recovery documentation, maintenance windows, or protection schedules for compute platforms.
bullets: Early design planning.
Expansion review.
Existing-system cleanup or validation.
Explaining tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions match the visible Backup Window Estimator inputs and explain how each value affects protection timing.

items.label: Total Dataset Size (TB)
items.description: The total source data footprint protected by the backup plan.
items.whyItMatters: Dataset size defines the upper bound of what may need to be protected, especially for full backups.
items.commonMistake: Sizing only around today's data and ignoring near-term growth.

items.label: Daily Change Rate (%)
items.description: The percentage of the dataset expected to change during the backup interval.
items.whyItMatters: Change rate controls incremental/differential backup size and can matter more than total capacity for recurring jobs.
items.commonMistake: Assuming daily backups only move a tiny amount without checking real change behavior.

items.label: Backup Type
items.description: The backup method being modeled, such as full, incremental, or differential behavior.
items.whyItMatters: Backup type determines how much data should be moved for each cycle.
items.commonMistake: Comparing backup products without matching the same job type.

items.label: Effective Throughput (MB/s)
items.description: The real sustained backup speed after source, target, network, protocol, and job behavior are considered.
items.whyItMatters: Effective throughput determines whether the job finishes inside the window.
items.commonMistake: Using link speed or disk peak speed as if it were real backup throughput.

items.label: Compression / Dedup (%)
items.description: The expected reduction in data transferred or stored because of compression or deduplication.
items.whyItMatters: Savings can help the window, but they may vary widely by data type and backup method.
items.commonMistake: Assuming deduplication always saves the same amount on every dataset.

items.label: Overhead (%)
items.description: Extra time or data impact from scanning, encryption, verification, snapshots, metadata, job startup, or operational inefficiency.
items.whyItMatters: Overhead can cause a backup to miss its window even when raw transfer math looks acceptable.
items.commonMistake: Ignoring all non-transfer work in the backup process.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means the backup should fit with reasonable margin. Watch means throughput, data growth, or job schedule should be reviewed. Risk means the backup may overrun the intended window or collide with recovery expectations.
bullets: Effective backup duration depends on real throughput, not theoretical speed.
Compression and deduplication help only when the dataset actually supports them.
A backup window should be compared against operations, maintenance, and recovery expectations.

sections.title: How this fits in the Compute flow
body: Backup Window is the final resilience check in the Compute flow because it tests whether the platform can be protected after sizing, performance, density, power, and recovery exposure are understood.
bullets: Use rebuild exposure from RAID Rebuild Time as recovery context.
Use storage and network throughput assumptions carefully.
A compute design is incomplete if it cannot be backed up in the required operating window.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Most compute planning problems come from trusting one comfortable number while ignoring the rest of the platform. CPU, RAM, storage, network, power, cooling, protection timing, and growth all interact.
bullets: Sizing only for average load instead of peak behavior.
Ignoring headroom, background tasks, maintenance windows, and growth.
Treating theoretical maximums as sustained real-world capacity.
Forgetting that one weak resource can make the rest of the system feel undersized.
Failing to document assumptions before hardware, licensing, or architecture decisions are locked in.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This guide explains the planning logic behind the tool. It does not replace manufacturer documentation, platform-specific limits, application requirements, benchmark testing, or project-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Vendor specifications still matter.
Application behavior may change the final requirement.
Site conditions, operating procedures, and maintenance expectations may affect the design.
A design with documented headroom is usually easier to support, troubleshoot, and grow.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal engineering review, manufacturer validation, application testing, compliance review, or project-specific design responsibility.
bullets: Validate against the actual hardware, platform, and software being deployed.
Confirm stakeholder expectations and operating requirements.
Document assumptions before implementation.
Use the results as decision support, not as a final authority.
